Pulse oximetry uses {{c1::red and infrared}} light transmitted through a translucent tissue bed to differentiate oxyHb from deoxyHb based on {{c2::differential light absorption}}.
column2: Signal is filtered to isolate pulsatile changes in light absorption.

column1: An SpO<sub>2</sub> of {{c1::<u>90%</u>}} corresponds to a PaO<sub>2</sub> of {{c2::<u>~60mmHg</u>}} — the shoulder of the oxyHb dissociation curve.
column2: Below this point, small ↓ SpO2 → large ↓ PaO2.

column1: A right shift of the oxyHb dissociation curve indicates {{c1::↓ Hb–O2 affinity}}, resulting in {{c2::↑ O2 delivery to tissues}}. Causes: ↑ H+, ↑ temp, ↑ PaCO2, ↑ 2,3-DPG.
During intraoperative monitoring, leads {{c1::II and V5}} are monitored concurrently to detect LV ischaemia.
Leads II and V5 together detect {{c1::>85%}} of ischaemic events affecting the LV during surgery.
column2: Lead V5 is the best lateral lead for the anterior and lateral left ventricle, lead II is best for RCA territory

column1: A CVP catheter estimates {{c1::right atrial and RV pressures}}, reflecting {{c2::RV preload}}.
column2: Less reliable as surrogate for LV filling or fluid responsiveness in isolation.

column1: The pulmonary artery wedge pressure (PCWP), also called PAOP, reflects {{c1::left atrial filling pressure}}.
column2: Measured by inflating balloon at PAC tip to occlude the proximal pulmonary artery and record distal pressure.

column1: Normal ETCO2 is {{c1::33–45mmHg}}.
During GA, the PaCO2–ETCO2 gradient is typically {{c1::~5mmHg}}.
column2: ETCO2 35mmHg ≈ PaCO2 40mmHg in absence of V/Q mismatch or sampling error.

column1: The BIS monitor provides a number on a scale of {{c1::0}} (isoelectric EEG) to {{c2::100}} (fully awake).
The target BIS value for general anaesthesia is {{c1::40–60}}.
The BIS monitor analyses the {{c1::EEG}} signal via a complex algorithm to produce a single number representing anaesthetic depth.
Train-of-four (TOF) consists of {{c1::4}} stimuli each separated by {{c2::0.5sec}}. The ratio of 4th to 1st twitch height = TOF ratio.
The TOF ratio is calculated as {{c1::T4/T1}} (height of 4th twitch divided by height of 1st twitch).
A TOF ratio of {{c1::<u><0.7</u>}} indicates {{c2::<u>non-depolarising block present</u>}}.
Adequate recovery from neuromuscular blockade is defined as a TOF ratio of {{c1::<u>≥0.9</u>}}.
MAP = {{c1::DBP + ⅓(SBP – DBP)::formula}}
Urine output under GA without a fluid load is {{c1::<u>negligible</u>}} — surgical stress ↑ aldosterone, ADH, renin–angiotensin II, and ANP → salt and water retention.
